_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Greenville County, SC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses that shape what genealogists can and cannot find.
display_name: Greenville County, South Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.fate: Early log and frame courthouse. Replaced as the county grew. No significant fire or war damage to early records.
Brick courthouse. Served until replaced by a larger building.
Survived the Civil War without destruction — Union forces did not occupy Greenville in significant numbers. Replaced in 1918.
Served until replaced by the current 1950 building.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or war damage
record_losses.description: Greenville County's courthouse survived the Civil War and has not experienced a significant fire. Records run continuously from the county's 1786 formation. This makes Greenville one of the best-preserved Upcountry counties for genealogical research. Gaps in the record are more likely to reflect the ancestor's circumstances — mill village residence (no deeds), poverty, or transience — than courthouse destruction.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Greenville County after 1786, the records almost certainly survive. Gaps in the paper trail point to the ancestor's circumstances — mill village life left fewer county records than farm life because mill workers owned no property and rarely appeared in court.
record_losses.source: SC Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records in the South Carolina State Archives.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Early deed books show some wear but remain legible.
deeds.repository: Greenville County Register of Deeds; SC Department of Archives and History microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com SC land records collection

wills_estates.completeness: Good — wills and estate records survive from near-formation
wills_estates.gaps: Loose estate papers from the earliest decades are fragmentary but bound volumes are intact.
wills_estates.repository: Greenville County Probate Court; SC Department of Archives and History
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; limited SC Archives digital collections

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage licenses and bonds survive from early period
marriages.gaps: Marriage bonds are more complete than registers before 1911. South Carolina did not require marriage licenses statewide until 1911.
marriages.repository: Greenville County Probate Court; SC Department of Archives and History microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — County Court minutes from near-formation
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps in the earliest volumes. District Court records (SC used a district court system until 1868) may predate county-level records for some types of proceedings.
court_minutes.repository: SC Department of Archives and History; Greenville County Clerk of Court
court_minutes.online_access: SC Archives microfilm; limited FamilySearch digitization

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until approximately 1920. Greenville city health department records may predate 1915 for the city proper.
vital_records.repository: SC Department of Health and Environmental Control (DHEC); SC Department of Archives and History for delayed certificates

substitute_sources.description: Greenville's textile mills — Woodside, Camperdown, Judson, Monaghan, and others — maintained employment records, payroll ledgers, and housing assignment logs. Where these records survive, they document workers' names, dates of employment, wages, and family members.
substitute_sources.repository: Greenville County Historical Society; South Carolina Historical Society; some records at SC Department of Archives and History. Furman University Special Collections holds some Greenville-area textile records.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for identifying textile worker families. Mill payroll records can establish dates of arrival in Greenville County when census records are ten years apart. Housing assignment records place families at specific addresses in specific mill villages. However, many mill company records were destroyed when mills closed or merged.
substitute_sources.source: Huff, Archie Vernon. Greenville: The History of the City and County in the South Carolina Piedmont. Columbia: University of South Carolina Press, 1995.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Agricultural schedules (1850–1880), manufacturing schedules (1820–1880), and slave schedules (1850–1860). Greenville County is enumerated from the first federal census.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families. Manufacturing schedules are particularly valuable for Greenville County given its early industrial development. Slave schedules (1850, 1860) list slaveholders and the age/sex/color of enslaved people but not their names.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Greenville County, SC.

substitute_sources.description: Baptist and Methodist churches dominated Greenville County's religious landscape. Church minute books, membership rolls, and baptism/marriage records supplement civil records, particularly before 1911 when SC marriage licenses became mandatory.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; SC Baptist Historical Collection at Furman University; United Methodist Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for documenting families between census years. Church transfer letters track migration between congregations and counties. Baptist church discipline records (excommunications, restorations) provide detail about individuals that civil records do not.
substitute_sources.source: SC Baptist Historical Collection finding aids, Furman University.

narrative_hooks: Greenville County's courthouse survived the Civil War and every decade since — {ancestor_name}'s deeds, wills, and court records almost certainly still exist. Gaps in the paper trail say more about the ancestor's circumstances than about courthouse losses.

If {ancestor_name} worked in a Greenville County textile mill, the courthouse may hold less about {pronoun_object} than the mill company's own records — payroll ledgers, housing assignments, and store accounts that documented daily life in ways the county recorder never did. Whether those company records survived the mill's closure is the key question.

Before 1911, South Carolina did not require marriage licenses, so a missing marriage record for {ancestor_name} in Greenville County may reflect the era rather than a common-law arrangement — check church records, particularly Baptist minute books.

South Carolina birth and death registration began in 1915, so the absence of a birth record for {ancestor_name} before that date is standard — census records, church records, and family Bible entries are the likeliest substitutes.
source_references: Huff, Archie Vernon. Greenville: The History of the City and County in the South Carolina Piedmont. Columbia: University of South Carolina Press, 1995.
SC Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records in the South Carolina State Archives.
SC Baptist Historical Collection finding aids, Furman University.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: South Carolina County Records. Columbia, 1937–1942.
